pattern: A matrix is valid exactly when every row and every column is a permutation of 1..n, so scanning each line and confirming it covers the full range catches any duplicate or missing value.
hints: Think about what property every valid row and every valid column must share -- there's really only one set of numbers each of them can represent.
For a row or column of length n, track which of the values 1..n you've seen so far; if by the end you haven't seen exactly n distinct values in that range, the line fails.
Check every row this way, then check every column the same way -- the matrix passes only if all of them independently satisfy the check.

edge_inputs.description: small valid matrix, a straightforward permutation in each row and column
smallest possible matrix, a single cell
every row is a valid permutation on its own, but a column repeats a value -- catches a solution that only checks rows
a nontrivial valid arrangement where every row and column is still a full permutation
largest allowed size, a cyclic shift pattern that stays valid in every row and column
a row itself repeats a value instead of covering the full range, so it must fail immediately
the first row is valid but the second row is not -- checking must not stop early on the first success

stress.description: random mix of cyclic-shift valid matrices and fully random matrices up to the max size
